explanation: The statement means that whenever 'prop_0' is true, then 'prop_1' should also be true at that moment (globally). This is the standard implication: globally, if 'prop_0' holds, then 'prop_1' must also hold.
The statement means that whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 should also be true at the same time. In LTL, this is usually written as 'globally, if prop_0 then prop_1'.

The requirement 'If the prop_0, it should prop_1' can be interpreted as whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 should also hold at that time. This corresponds to the safety property 'Globally, if prop_0 then prop_1', written as G (prop_0 -> prop_1).

This statement expresses that whenever prop_0 holds, then prop_1 should also hold at that same point in time. In LTL, this type of implication, which must be enforced at all times, is written as 'Globally, if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 holds'.
The statement says that whenever 'prop_0' is true, 'prop_1' should also hold simultaneously. In LTL, this is expressed as 'globally, if prop_0, then prop_1': G (prop_0 -> prop_1).

This expresses that whenever 'prop_0' holds at a point in time, 'prop_1' should also hold at that point. In LTL, this can be captured by saying 'globally, if prop_0 then prop_1', so prop_0 -> prop_1 is always true.
The statement says that whenever 'prop_0' holds, then 'prop_1' should also hold. In LTL, this is captured by saying globally, 'prop_0' implies 'prop_1'.

The requirement 'If prop_0, it should prop_1' means that whenever prop_0 is true, then prop_1 should also be true at that point. In LTL, this is expressed globally: G (prop_0 -> prop_1). This guarantees that at all times if prop_0 occurs, then prop_1 must also occur at that same step.

The property 'If prop_0, it should prop_1' can be interpreted as: globally, whenever prop_0 holds, then prop_1 should also hold at the same moment. This corresponds to the LTL formula: globally (prop_0 implies prop_1).

The statement says that whenever prop_0 occurs, then prop_1 should also occur (either immediately or at some point in the future, but at least once after prop_0). The standard way to say this in LTL is: globally, if prop_0 holds, then eventually prop_1 will hold.
